topic: Alignment - SIMD Alignment Requirements and Aligned Buffers

turns.content: I'm trying to port a performance-critical image filter using std::arch intrinsics, but I keep hitting runtime panics from misaligned memory access. We have a huge dataset of RGB pixels stored in Vec<u8> and we need to process chunks of 128 bytes per SIMD lane. How do I ensure the data is actually aligned to 64-byte boundaries before calling these functions?

The issue is that standard Vec<u8> doesn't guarantee alignment, so you cannot pass it directly into intrinsics like __builtin_packed_unpack. Think of SIMD aligned buffers as a high-speed rail line where trains (your data) must arrive on perfectly straight tracks; if the track isn't level (aligned), the train crashes (the panic). To fix this, you should use MaybeUninit<u8> with repr(align(64)) to create an uninitialised buffer that is guaranteed to be 64-byte aligned without running the initialization cost until needed. Alternatively, allocate a fixed-size array like [u8; 128] using repr(align(64)), which acts like pre-laid tracks you can load data onto directly. You must check alignment before calling intrinsics by testing ptr::align_of::<u8>() on your pointer and ensuring it matches the requirement, otherwise the CPU will fault.

topic: Alignment - SIMD Alignment Requirements and Aligned Buffers

turns.content: Hey, I'm trying to build a proof-of-concept for using SIMD instructions in Rust. But I'm running into alignment issues. Here's my code snippet:

That sounds exciting! So, you want to use SIMD (Single Instruction Multiple Data) operations? That’s great because it can significantly speed up your computations. One important thing to remember is that many modern CPU instructions require data to be aligned on specific boundaries—typically 16, 32, or 64 bytes for AVX, SSE, and AVX-512 respectively. If you pass unaligned memory to SIMD intrinsics, it’ll crash or produce incorrect results.
I see. So I should make sure my buffers are aligned before calling the SIMD functions?

Absolutely! One way to handle this is by allocating an array with a specific alignment, like `[u8; 64]` with `repr(align(64))`. This guarantees that your buffer starts on a 64-byte boundary. Then you can pass the pointer to the SIMD functions safely.
But if I allocate an array like `[u8; 64]` with `repr(align(64))`, won’t it always be aligned? What if I get data from somewhere else?

Good point! Sometimes the buffer you receive might not be aligned. In that case, you should check its alignment before calling SIMD functions. You can use `core::arch::x86_64::_mm256_testc_si256` or similar tests to verify alignment. If it’s unaligned, you’ll have to either reallocate a new buffer and copy the data there (making sure it’s aligned), or find an alternative approach.
